metaTitle: Contract Breach & Specific Performance Bangladesh Guide
title: Contract Breach and Specific Performance Suit in Bangladesh: A Complete Legal Guide
metaDescription: Complete guide to contract breach and specific performance suits in Bangladesh — legal remedies, court procedure, the Specific Relief Act 1877, and how to win your case.
heroIntro: When a party to a contract fails to fulfil their obligations, the innocent party has a range of legal remedies available under Bangladesh law. The most powerful of these is the suit for specific performance — a court order compelling the breaching party to perform their contractual obligations. Understanding when specific performance is available, how to file a civil suit, and what defences can be raised is essential for anyone dealing with a broken contract in Bangladesh.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Contract Breach & Specific Performance Bangladesh
points: Specific performance is governed by the Specific Relief Act, 1877 in Bangladesh.
Courts can order the breaching party to perform the contract rather than just pay damages.
It is most commonly granted in contracts for sale of immovable property (land, flat).
Specific performance is discretionary — courts can refuse it in certain circumstances.
File the suit before the Civil Court (District Judge or Sub-Judge's Court) where the property is situated or the contract was to be performed.

sections.h2: What Is Breach of Contract Under Bangladesh Law?
sections.content:
A contract is a legally binding agreement between two or more parties. Under the Contract Act, 1872 — which applies in Bangladesh — a contract is enforceable if it is made by competent parties, for lawful consideration, with free consent, and for a lawful object.

A breach of contract occurs when one party fails to perform their obligations under the contract without legal justification. Breaches may be:

  • Actual breach: The party fails to perform when performance is due (e.g., a seller fails to hand over possession of land after receiving the sale price).
  • Anticipatory breach: A party clearly indicates, before the performance date, that they will not perform (e.g., a developer tells a buyer they are cancelling the flat purchase agreement).
  • Partial breach: A party performs only part of their obligations (e.g., supplying only half the agreed goods).
  • Fundamental breach: A breach so serious that it goes to the root of the contract and justifies the innocent party treating the contract as terminated.

In Bangladesh, the most common contract disputes involve:

  • Sale agreements for land (baina deed disputes);
  • Construction contracts between developers and buyers;
  • Commercial supply and service agreements;
  • Employment contracts;
  • Franchise and joint venture agreements.

sections.h2: Legal Remedies for Breach of Contract in Bangladesh
sections.content:
When a contract is breached, the innocent party has several legal remedies under Bangladesh law:

  1. Specific Performance: A court order compelling the breaching party to perform the contract as agreed. This is the most direct remedy and is particularly valuable where money damages cannot adequately compensate the loss (e.g., a unique piece of land).
  2. Damages: Monetary compensation for the loss suffered as a result of the breach. Damages can be ordinary (flowing naturally from the breach), special (arising from special circumstances known to both parties), or nominal (where a breach occurred but no actual loss is proved).
  3. Injunction: A court order restraining the breaching party from doing something that would violate the contract — for example, preventing a seller from selling the land to a third party while the specific performance suit is pending.
  4. Rescission: Cancellation of the contract, releasing both parties from further obligations. If the innocent party suffers a loss from rescission, they can also claim restitution.
  5. Quantum Meruit: Recovery of the reasonable value of work done or services rendered under a partially performed contract.

The choice of remedy depends on the nature of the contract, the circumstances of the breach, and what the innocent party most needs. An experienced civil and corporate lawyer can advise on the optimal strategy for your case.

sections.h2: Specific Performance: The Specific Relief Act 1877
sections.content:
The primary statute governing specific performance in Bangladesh is the Specific Relief Act, 1877. This Act provides the legal framework for obtaining court orders compelling a party to perform their contractual obligations rather than merely paying damages.

Key provisions of the Specific Relief Act relevant to Bangladesh litigation:

  • Section 9: Courts can enforce specific performance of a contract where standard money compensation is inadequate.
  • Section 10: Contracts for the transfer of immovable property (land, apartments, buildings) are presumed to be cases where money compensation is inadequate — making specific performance the appropriate remedy in most property cases.
  • Section 11: Specific performance of contracts connected with trusts and other fiduciary relationships can be ordered.
  • Section 12: Part performance — where the court can order performance of the part of the contract that can be performed, along with compensation for the remainder if full performance is impossible.
  • Section 14: Lists the types of contracts that cannot be specifically enforced (see the next section).
  • Section 21: Provides for the court to award damages in addition to, or instead of, specific performance in appropriate cases.

For land disputes, the Specific Relief Act is the most important legal tool. If you have paid an advance (baina) for land and the seller is refusing to execute the registered deed, a specific performance suit under the Specific Relief Act is the correct legal remedy. Contact a land lawyer in Bangladesh immediately to protect your rights.

sections.h2: When Courts Will and Won't Grant Specific Performance
sections.content:
Specific performance is a discretionary remedy — even if you have a strong legal case, the court may decline to grant it in certain circumstances. Understanding when courts grant and refuse specific performance helps in assessing your case realistically.

Courts WILL typically grant specific performance when:

  • The contract is for the sale of immovable property (land, flat, building);
  • Money damages would be an inadequate remedy (e.g., the property is unique or sentimental);
  • The plaintiff has been ready, willing, and able to perform their own obligations;
  • The plaintiff files the suit within the limitation period (3 years from date of breach for most contract suits).

Courts WON'T grant specific performance when:

  • The contract involves personal services (courts will not compel a person to work for another);
  • The contract terms are uncertain or ambiguous;
  • Performance would be impossible due to changed circumstances;
  • The plaintiff is in breach of their own obligations;
  • Third parties have acquired bona fide rights in the property without notice of the prior contract (e.g., an innocent purchaser);
  • The plaintiff delayed unreasonably in filing suit (laches);
  • Performance would be inequitable or oppressive given the circumstances.

Courts balance the equities of both parties. Preparing a strong factual record of your readiness and willingness to perform is critical to success. Consult a land and civil lawyer to evaluate whether your case qualifies for specific performance.

sections.h2: How to File a Suit for Specific Performance in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Filing a specific performance suit in Bangladesh follows the civil court procedure under the Code of Civil Procedure (CPC), 1908. The steps are:

  1. Engage a Civil Lawyer: Retain an experienced civil and land lawyer to draft the plaint and conduct the suit.
  2. Draft the Plaint: The plaint must contain: the parties' details, the contract and its terms, the breach, the plaintiff's readiness and willingness to perform, and the prayers (relief sought — specifically performance plus any supplementary relief).
  3. Attach Documents: Include the original contract/baina deed, proof of payment of advance, any correspondence showing the defendant's refusal to perform, and evidence of the plaintiff's readiness to perform (e.g., bank statement showing funds available).
  4. File at the Correct Court: For immovable property, file at the Civil Court (Sub-Judge's Court or District Judge's Court) where the property is situated. For other contracts, file where the contract was to be performed.
  5. Pay Court Fees: Court fees are payable based on the value of the subject matter (usually the contract price).
  6. Injunction Application: Simultaneously file an application for a temporary injunction (TI) to restrain the defendant from selling or transferring the property to anyone else pending the hearing of the suit.
  7. Summons and Written Statement: The court serves summons on the defendant, who files a written statement (defence).
  8. Evidence: Both parties file affidavit evidence and cross-examine witnesses.
  9. Judgment and Decree: The court passes a decree for specific performance, directing the defendant to execute and register the sale deed. If they refuse, the court can execute the deed itself or through a court official.

sections.h2: Defences Against a Specific Performance Suit
sections.content:
A defendant facing a specific performance suit can raise several valid defences:

  • No Valid Contract: Argue that the alleged contract was never concluded — for example, there was no consensus on essential terms, the contract lacked consideration, or it was void for illegality.
  • Plaintiff in Breach: If the plaintiff (buyer) failed to pay the full purchase price or failed to perform their own obligations under the contract, the defendant can resist specific performance.
  • Limitation: A specific performance suit must be filed within the limitation period (generally 3 years from the date of the breach or refusal to perform). A suit filed after this period is barred by limitation.
  • Laches: Even within the limitation period, unreasonable delay by the plaintiff that prejudices the defendant may lead the court to refuse specific performance in equity.
  • Impossibility: If performing the contract has become impossible (e.g., the property has been acquired by the government, or destroyed), specific performance cannot be ordered.
  • Bona Fide Purchaser: If the defendant sold the property to a third-party buyer who purchased without knowledge of the plaintiff's prior contract, the third party's rights may prevail — especially if they registered their title before the plaintiff's suit.
  • Fraud or Misrepresentation: If the plaintiff induced the contract through fraud or material misrepresentation, the contract is voidable and specific performance should be refused.

sections.h2: Damages vs. Specific Performance: Which Is Better for You?
sections.content:
Choosing between claiming damages and seeking specific performance requires careful legal and practical analysis. Here is a comparative overview to help you decide:

  • Specific Performance is better when: The subject matter is unique (e.g., a particular plot of land, an ancestral property, or a rare asset); when money cannot adequately compensate your loss; when you genuinely want the contract performed rather than money; or when the property's value has increased significantly since the contract was signed.
  • Damages are better when: The subject matter is easily replaceable in the market; when you have already mitigated your loss by finding an alternative; when the specific performance suit would take many years while you have urgent cash flow needs; or when the defendant is insolvent and a money decree can be enforced against assets.
  • Both together: Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act allows the court to award damages in addition to specific performance where the defendant's delay caused loss even before the decree was passed. Claiming both gives you maximum protection.

A practical note: specific performance suits in Bangladesh can take 3–7 years at the trial court level, with further appeals. An experienced civil lawyer in Dhaka will help you weigh the timeline against your commercial interests before deciding on your litigation strategy.

sections.h2: Practical Tips for Land and Property Contract Disputes
sections.content:
Land contract disputes are by far the most common setting for specific performance suits in Bangladesh. Here are practical tips to protect yourself:

  • Always Use a Registered Agreement: A baina (advance payment) agreement for land purchase should be registered before the Sub-Registrar to give it legal validity and to create a public record of your rights.
  • Retain All Payment Records: Keep bank transfer receipts, demand drafts, pay orders, or cash payment acknowledgements for every payment made to the seller.
  • File an Injunction Early: As soon as the seller shows signs of backing out, apply for an injunction to prevent them from registering the deed in favour of a third party. Once a bona fide purchaser registers their title, your specific performance suit becomes much harder to succeed.
  • Check the Property's Title Before Buying: Always conduct a thorough title search through a land lawyer before signing any agreement. Prior encumbrances, court orders, or disputed ownership can defeat your claim even if your contract is valid.
  • Act Promptly: The 3-year limitation period runs from the date of breach. Do not wait — seek legal advice immediately when the seller signals non-performance.

faqs.question: What is the limitation period for filing a specific performance suit in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Under the Limitation Act, 1908, a suit for specific performance of a contract must generally be filed within 3 years from the date fixed for the performance, or if no date is fixed, from the date the plaintiff first heard that the defendant refused to perform. Missing this deadline means your suit will be barred by limitation and dismissed.

faqs.question: Can I get an injunction to stop the seller from selling the land while my case is pending?
faqs.answer: Yes. You can file an application for a temporary injunction (TI) simultaneously with your specific performance suit. The court can grant an interim injunction (ex parte or after hearing both parties) restraining the defendant from selling, mortgaging, or otherwise encumbering the property pending the final decision of the suit.

faqs.question: What if the seller registers the land to a third party before I file my case?
faqs.answer: If the third-party purchaser had actual or constructive notice of your prior contract (e.g., through your registered baina deed), your specific performance suit can still succeed against both the seller and the third party. However, if the third party was a bona fide purchaser without notice, your rights may be defeated. This is why filing early and registering your baina agreement is critical.

faqs.question: How long does a specific performance suit take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: A specific performance suit at the trial court level typically takes 3 to 7 years in Bangladesh, depending on the complexity, the number of witnesses, and court workload. Appeals to the High Court and Appellate Division can add further years. Interim injunctions provide protection during this period.

faqs.question: Can I claim both specific performance and damages in the same suit?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under Section 21 of the Specific Relief Act 1877, the court can award damages in addition to specific performance where the breach caused loss before the decree was passed. Many plaintiffs claim specific performance as the primary relief and damages as an alternative or supplementary relief in the same plaint.
